data.epi_content:
I couldn't help thinking that I might have become accustomed to death without realizing it.

When I was young, all I had seen was my father who passed away after becoming disillusioned with the world—just that one experience.

Vivian realized that witnessing someone who was alive just moments ago dying—especially when the cause was suicide—was quite painful for the observer, even if they couldn't fully empathize with the person taking their own life.

Unlike cold weapons like swords or spears that require a person to swing, thrust, and cut, firearms that resolve everything with just one simple choice through mechanical means could easily slaughter people without being swayed by petty compassion.

Though it was a pistol with a small caliber and not particularly powerful, it was clearly sufficient to kill a person—the clicking sound of the trigger followed almost simultaneously by the explosion, and then the red blood splattering against the wall.

Vivian watched in slow motion as a hole formed in the girl's head, brain matter slightly protruding, blood spraying onto the wall, and then rushed toward the girl who had been pointing the gun at her just moments ago.

If she had wanted to shoot, she could have done so at any time.

It seemed the girl hated Vivian, but not enough to kill her.

When she shot at Lydia, she pulled the trigger without the slightest hesitation.

But facing Vivian, she hesitated for a long while, and in the end, instead of pulling the trigger against her, she blew her own head off.

If only she had shot at Vivian instead, she could have deployed a defensive spell to block it.

She would have had to subdue Erica somewhat forcefully, but at least the only person who would have died would have been that woman Lydia, who appeared to be tormenting Erica.

Vivian grabbed the girl's body, realized she was still alive, and called for the most skilled magician she knew to treat her.

But could someone really survive something like that?

Even though Vivian had skimmed through her studies except for magic and swordsmanship, she knew very well that once a person's head was damaged, it couldn't be restored—no matter if you brought a priest or a grand magician.

Nevertheless, unable to give up on Erica, she could only watch as Erica's body seemed to be healing, until the Crown Prince stepped in and ordered the treatment to stop.

That's when Vivian made a choice that even a duke couldn't imagine—grabbing the Crown Prince by the collar.

If it had been someone like Evan who tried to grab his collar, their wrist would have been severed immediately, and they would have been dragged away to be thrown into an underground prison.

"What on earth are you doing?!"

"Vivian, that would be better off dead."

Hearing those words, she shed tears as she realized what would follow.

"That's just a piece of meat.

A piece of meat in the form of Erica Mecklenburg."

In truth, the Crown Prince didn't need to step forward and say such things.

He was often criticized for being cold and heartless toward others, and Vivian knew deep down that the Crown Prince had feelings for her.

This wasn't about winning Vivian's favor, but rather showing consideration for Vivian as a person.

Because she wouldn't be able to give up on that body that barely clung to life.

She was too compassionate, and for some reason, seemed to regard Erica as someone special.

The Crown Prince didn't understand Vivian, but nonetheless, he handled the matter directly to prevent her from falling into misfortune by indulging her childish whims.

He stepped in himself, forcing the decision to give up on Erica's life.

Both Evan and the Crown Prince had already accepted that Erica was dead, saying that her physical body should be discarded—either nailed in a coffin and buried somewhere, or cremated and placed in an urn.

And not long after, with healing magic cut off and blood gushing out, Erica breathed her last.

Her faint breathing stopped, and her trembling arms and legs went limp.
data.epi_content2:
But Vivian couldn't express the knot in her heart.

She didn't understand love, or the considerations between nobles.

That is, she knew intellectually but physically detested the practice of freely manipulating what could be controlled—like capturing slaves who fled to neighboring territories, executing commoners who eloped with daughters of noble families, or "accidentally" stabbing honorable knights in the neck during competitions with neighboring territories because they couldn't be directly dismissed.

That was the source of her father's mental illness.

Evan seemed completely insane.

The Crown Prince had long since become twisted enough to easily give up human lives.

Though they say power doesn't wear a human face, this was too alien to rule over humans and reign above them.

Just as a cat might bring a mouse from outside as a gift to its owner, but the owner wouldn't happily accept it.

Evan seemed to seriously believe what Erica had said before dying—about going back in time after death.

He was muttering, as if brainwashing himself.

The Crown Prince looked at Evan with interest, speaking as if he would provide everything he wanted.

To be precise, Erica's head from her corpse that the imperial shadow had just taken away—her head that would now be pulverized.

So she quietly uttered:

"You're all madmen."

"Vivian, go to your room and rest for a while.

The reason I'm fond of you is because of traits like that, and I'd hate for you to lose them."

If this hadn't happened—if Erica hadn't blown her head off in front of Evan—he would have cut off the Crown Prince's flirtatious remarks.

Though whether they were actually flirtatious was questionable.

Evan now blinked once as if such things were no longer important, bowed once to the Crown Prince, and returned to his room.

His interests had shifted to magic, the girl, and her head.

……………………

The next day, a note slipped under Evan's door.

He picked it up, read it, and began heading somewhere.

Classes, of course, wouldn't be held after such a horrific incident the day before.

He walked for a long time, leaving the Academy and heading toward the cemetery.

And as he was passing through the main gate to catch a carriage, Vivian, dressed in black, was waiting for him.

"...You're going to check on Erica, aren't you?"

Vivian couldn't bring herself to ask if he was going to open her skull and fiddle with her brain.

Despite witnessing death right before her eyes, she was excessively proper.

"Yes."

"Can't you just leave it be?

It's already terribly..."

"If Erica is really dead.
data.epi_content3:
If what I'm imagining is just the delusion of a madman.

If that's the case, let's make the same assumption.

Evan continued speaking rapidly.

So quickly that one might doubt he was even breathing.

From his appearance, he seemed to genuinely believe Erica was alive.

"Then I killed Erica.

That day when you were slapped, Erica's family completely fell apart.

The eldest son of the family had already died, and the head of the family died in an accident too, though you know.

On such a day, I went looking for Erica just to win your favor and comfort you.

I was going to get angry, but as we talked, she didn't show any sign of pain.

At her feet, a large piece of glass was embedded, causing a huge wound with blood streaming out.

But she just smiled as if nothing was wrong."

"I was so shocked I don't even remember what I said.

Anyway, as we continued talking, Erica asked me to save her.

I didn't know this is what she meant by saving her. I just told her to call me if things got difficult and gave her a cheap jeweled ring with a magical communication signal."

It seemed a bit unfair to call the jewel "cheap," but Evan had enough wealth to acquire hundreds of such jewels.

"And I never visited her myself.

Today, I went to see her. And in the end, what happened?

She blew her own head off.

That makes me her killer.

So Erica must be alive.

I need to find evidence that she's alive."

Evan grabbed Vivian's shoulders with crazed eyes as he spoke.

An ordinary person might have been intimidated and frightened by his intensity, but Vivian just sighed and said again:

"Evan, you really are insane."

"Perhaps."

"Take me with you."

"Why?"

"Because I can't leave a madman alone with a corpse."

"Suit yourself."

"Thank you."

Vivian wasn't particularly surprised that Evan's attitude toward her had completely changed.

After her father passed away, the knight who had seemed so stiff and frightening whenever they met would hold her and try his best not to appear scary, imitating a lightness and cuteness that didn't suit him at all.

Perhaps death truly is the best way to change a person.

Riding in the swaying carriage, Vivian pondered this thought.

Only after death did Evan begin to shift his gaze from her to Erica.

But in Vivian's opinion, that didn't seem like a particularly worthwhile death.
